label: SES-050 reference book entity schema designed (audit-and-finalize): 1 session, 6 decisions (DEC-135..140), 0 planning items, 7 references

session.title: Reference book entity schema designed; third per-entity governance schema specification produced (audit-and-finalize)
session.conversation_reference: Claude.ai audit-and-finalize conversation for the reference_book entity schema — third per-entity schema-design conversation in the governance entity schema-design workstream. Opened against PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-reference-book.md. Discovered on the first pre-flight check that the kickoff's deliverable (PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/reference_book.md, 467 lines) had already been drafted and committed by a previous Claude.ai sandbox session at c1d007e on 05-21-26 14:55, but that prior conversation never authored a session record or a close-out payload. The state mismatch between the local sandbox clone (one commit ahead of origin) and the V2 database (no SES-050 or DEC-135..140 yet recorded) surfaced the gap. The conversation transitioned from schema-design mode to audit-and-finalize mode: a structured audit pass against the schema spec guide §7.1 completeness gate and §6 cross-spec consistency requirements, against the workstream master plan's position-3-of-6 constraints, against DEC-117's family-1 classification, and against the predecessor specs' (workstream.md, conversation.md) declarations the spec relies on. The audit concluded the spec is substantively complete, methodologically disciplined, and passes every completeness and consistency criterion — with one factual error appearing in three places (the Change Log v1.0 paragraph, the DEC-138 description in §3.9.1, and the DEC-117 entry in §3.9.3) that misidentified the three `reference_book_kind` enum values that are additions beyond DEC-117's seven. Doug accepted the audit and authorized executing the Path 1 correction-and-close-out plan: fix the three text errors (substituting `architecture_document`, `conduct_framework`, `investigation_report` for the wrongly-named `workstream_master_plan`, `apply_script`, `session_startup_document` — those three are explicitly in DEC-117's seven per the DEC-117 decision record), bump the spec to v1.1 with Revision Control and Change Log entries documenting the correction, then author the close-out payload and apply prompt for SES-050. The conversation also surfaced a class-level prevention question: the state mismatch happened because the prior sandbox session committed the spec but never ran the close-out apply, leaving git and the V2 database silently out of sync. Doug asked for a prevention recommendation; the response named a workstream-state reconciliation utility (`tools/workstream_reconcile.py`) invoked from every kickoff prompt's pre-flight that compares the workstream's git state against the V2 database state and surfaces any committed-but-not-applied deliverables before architectural discussion begins. No planning item authored for the reconciliation utility in this payload; that question is flagged at the end of the close-out and Doug decides whether to add it. No transcript preserved per DEC-025.
session.topics_covered:
Seed prompt: "Design the `reference_book` entity type schema for V2's storage layer. Drive a structured architectural discussion that produces one deliverable: PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/governance-schema-specs/reference_book.md — the complete schema specification per the template in governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md. Cadence matches the methodology workstream's schema-design conversations and the user-interface planning conversations: structured architectural discussion driven one decision at a time, building toward the specification section by section. At conversation close: decisions written via direct API as decision records; any deferred items written as planning items; one session record written by Doug through the V2 desktop New Session dialog per the session-record-at-close pattern."

Topics worked through:

1. Pre-flight orientation. Sparse-cloned the crmbuilder repo state at session open. Confirmed `crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md` is the session-startup entry per Doug's confirmation. On the first `git log` check, found that the local sandbox clone is one commit ahead of origin/main: c1d007e "v2: reference book schema spec — third of six governance entity schemas" dated 05-21-26 14:55 by author Doug, adding a complete 467-line `reference_book.md` spec. Cross-checked the V2 db-export: decisions through DEC-134, sessions through SES-049, no DEC-135..140 or SES-050 yet. Conclusion: a prior Claude.ai sandbox session drafted and committed the spec but never authored a session record or close-out payload; the V2 database is silently out of sync with the local git state. Surfaced this immediately to Doug.

2. Prevention discussion. Doug asked how to prevent the class of issue (sandbox commits spec → never closes out → V2 app shows stale workstream state → next conversation starts from stale assumption). Response framed the root cause as a two-system-of-record gap (git holds committed deliverables; V2 database holds governance state; the bridge — manual close-out apply — is a separate step that can be skipped). Recommended: a workstream-state reconciliation check at every kickoff pre-flight. Concretely a small `tools/workstream_reconcile.py` that takes a workstream identifier, asks the V2 API for that workstream's conversations and their statuses, asks git for commits touching the workstream's deliverable directory, and cross-references — any commit whose deliverable is not referenced by some session record's artifacts_produced is a stale-state signal. Alternatives considered (automatic close-out from the sandbox, V2 desktop app `last_processed_commit_hash` warnings, co-commit hooks) and rejected as either infeasible (sandbox can't reach Doug's local API) or higher-effort with smaller coverage. Flagged at conversation close for Doug's planning-item decision — not added to this payload.

3. Path selection. Three paths presented: (1) adopt the existing c1d007e commit and close out; (2) discard the commit and redo schema design from scratch; (3) audit-first then choose. Recommended Path 3. Doug accepted Path 3.

4. Audit execution. Read crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md, governance-schema-workstream-plan.md, governance-entity-schema-spec-guide.md, governance-schema-specs/workstream.md (predecessor 1), governance-schema-specs/conversation.md (predecessor 2), DEC-117 from db-export/decisions.json, sessions SES-048 and SES-049 from db-export/sessions.json, plus the spec file itself in full. Audited against four dimensions: schema-spec methodology guide §7.1 per-spec completeness gate (all nine §3 subsections present and substantively filled, §3.7 ≥10 testable criteria, §3.8 explicit category tags, §3.9 cites required references); §6 cross-spec consistency requirements (identifier prefix style, format, status field naming, status values, default starter, relationship-kind naming, field naming, timestamp inheritance with explicit deviation documented, soft-delete, identifier auto-assignment, API envelope); workstream master plan position-3-of-6 and constraints (RB-NNN working assumption realized, version history and 'in force at' semantics realized); predecessor-spec consistency (workstream.md line 123 declares `workstream_planned_in_reference_book`, verified; conversation.md generic-kinds posture for conversation→reference_book, verified).

5. Audit findings. Conclusion: the spec passes the per-spec completeness gate on every criterion and conforms to the §6 cross-spec consistency requirements with one explicit, justified deviation (base timestamps only for documentary-shaped lifecycles, established as a new cross-spec precedent in §1 and DEC-137). The spec also establishes a well-motivated parent-plus-child versioning model that diverges from charter/status's singleton-with-payload pattern with rationale (file content lives in git/files, database tracks metadata only). One substantive factual error identified: the Change Log v1.0 paragraph at line 21, the DEC-138 description at line 420, and the DEC-117 entry in §3.9.3 at line 438 each named the three `reference_book_kind` enum values that are additions beyond DEC-117 as `workstream_master_plan`, `apply_script`, `session_startup_document`. Per the DEC-117 decision record's verbatim text, those three values are explicitly listed in DEC-117's seven artifacts. The actual three additions beyond DEC-117 — present in the eleven-value enum but absent from DEC-117's seven — are `architecture_document`, `conduct_framework`, `investigation_report`. Doug accepted the audit and authorized Path 1 (correct-and-close-out).

6. Correction execution. Five str_replace operations on `governance-schema-specs/reference_book.md`: (a) update header `Last Updated` to 05-21-26 15:30; (b) bump header `Status` to `Draft v1.1` with annotation; (c) append a v1.1 row to the Revision Control table documenting the correction; (d) prepend a v1.1 paragraph to the Change Log section documenting the correction and update the v1.0 paragraph to be factually correct (architecture_document, conduct_framework, investigation_report); (e) correct DEC-138 description in §3.9.1; (f) correct DEC-117 entry in §3.9.3. All five operations verified — the enum declaration at line 96 (originally line 93 pre-edit) was correct in v1.0 and is unchanged. The two surviving mentions of the wrongly-named triple are now in the v1.1 Revision Control row and v1.1 Change Log paragraph, where they document the correction itself, which is the correct location.

7. Close-out payload and apply prompt authored. This payload (ses_050.json) and the companion apply prompt (CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-apply-close-out-ses-050.md) drafted in one pass. Identifier check: latest applied SES is SES-049, so SES-050 is next-available; latest DEC is DEC-134, so DEC-135..140 is next-available for the six decisions; no new planning items in this payload (PI-022 covers retroactive backfill including this conversation's records implicitly; the reconciliation-utility planning item is flagged at close-out for Doug's decision but not added).

8. Decisions authored (DEC-135..140 — see the decisions array below):
   - DEC-135: `reference_book` identifier prefix and format — RB-NNN, two-letter form acceptable per DEC-123, kickoff's open-question concern about visual ambiguity resolved by checking remaining-three-conversations' working prefixes (WT, COP, DEP) for confirmed non-collision.
   - DEC-136: `reference_book` versioning model — parent record plus child versions table. Distinct from charter/status's singleton-with-payload pattern because the file content lives in git/files and the database tracks version metadata only. Denormalized current-version pointer fields on the parent stay in sync with child rows via access-layer recompute on any child-table write. Establishes the documentary-entity versioning pattern for the project.
   - DEC-137: `reference_book` documentary-shaped lifecycle with three statuses and base timestamps only. active → archived / superseded transition map with the truly-terminal posture inherited verbatim from workstream and the supersession-requires-edge access-layer rule mirroring workstream's pattern. Establishes new cross-spec precedent: documentary-shaped lifecycles inherit base timestamps only, deviating from DEC-126's per-status-timestamps-for-workflow-shapes precedent. The remaining three governance specs apply the documentary-vs-workflow distinction on their own facts.
   - DEC-138: `reference_book_kind` closed enum with eleven values — covering all seven of DEC-117's named artifact types (product_requirements_document, implementation_plan, workstream_master_plan, methodology_guide, schema_specification, apply_script, session_startup_document) plus three additional types observed in the project's operating history beyond DEC-117 (architecture_document, conduct_framework, investigation_report) plus an `other` sentinel. The seven-versus-three split is documented honestly per the v1.1 correction.
   - DEC-139: `reference_book` field inventory plus file-path semantics plus engagement-scoping plus soft-delete posture. Seven content/classification fields plus three file-pointer fields plus three inherited base timestamps (no per-status timestamps per DEC-137). Repo-relative file path with no leading slash and no `..` segments; resolved at use time against the consuming repository. No engagement-scoping flag (per V2's per-engagement isolation, scoping is implicit). Default soft-delete with restore, distinct from `archived` and `superseded` status outcomes.
   - DEC-140: `reference_book` API surface plus version-management sub-endpoints plus master-pane Kind column plus default UI with inline version-history plus sixteen acceptance criteria. Standard 8-endpoint set for the parent plus three version-management sub-endpoints (GET /reference-books/{id}/versions, POST /reference-books/{id}/versions, GET /reference-books/{id}/version-at?as_of=...) realizing the in-force-at-time-T semantics named in the kickoff. List-endpoint filters `kind` and `status` alongside `include_deleted`. Master-pane Kind column as third column. Inline version-history section in the detail pane with Add Version affordance. Sixteen acceptance criteria captured.

Establishes the reference_book entity type for V2 storage with seven content/classification fields plus two denormalized version-pointer columns plus three inherited base timestamps (no per-status lifecycle timestamps — new cross-spec precedent for documentary-shaped lifecycles, DEC-137). session.in_flight_at_end:
Next workstream conversation: work_ticket entity schema design (fourth per-entity schema-design conversation, of six). Opens against PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/schema-design-kickoff-work-ticket.md. That conversation produces governance-schema-specs/work_ticket.md and inherits the five cross-spec precedents now in force: the three from workstream (references-edge over foreign-key for parent-child governance relationships, per-status lifecycle timestamps for workflow-shaped lifecycles, terminal-states-are-terminal discipline), the one from conversation (typed sequencing edges introduced when entity-family frequency justifies), and the one new precedent from this conversation (documentary-shaped lifecycles inherit base timestamps only — workflow-shape per-status timestamps remain the default for workflow-shaped lifecycles, which work_ticket likely is). The work_ticket entity is structurally most directly related to reference_book through its anticipated inbound `read_first`-style edges to reference books named in a kickoff prompt's read-list, and through its outbound consumption-by-conversation edge already declared as `work_ticket_consumed_by_conversation` (tentative) in conversation.md section 3.3.2. After work_ticket.md lands, two more schema-design conversations follow in workstream order (close_out_payload, deposit_event). After all six schema specifications exist, the build-planning conversation opens against governance-schema-build-planning-kickoff.md and produces the integrating Product Requirements Document, implementation plan, per-slice build prompts, and the consolidated vocab.py update aggregating relationship-kind additions from all six specs. Multi-tenancy routing fix slice B and the Cleveland Business Mentors redo Phase 1 conversation continue in parallel as independent workstreams per DEC-122.

Open-and-flagged for Doug's planning-item decision (not in this payload): authorize a workstream-state reconciliation utility (tools/workstream_reconcile.py) invoked from every kickoff prompt's pre-flight, plus the matching one-line additions to crmbuilder/CLAUDE.md and the kickoff-prompt template, to prevent the class of state-drift that produced this conversation's audit-and-finalize posture. The mechanism would surface any committed-but-not-applied workstream deliverable at session start, before architectural discussion begins. If accepted as a planning item, it would land at the next close-out (SES-051) rather than retroactively in this one.

decisions.title: reference_book entity identifier prefix and format: RB-NNN, two-letter form, no collision with REF or remaining-three working prefixes
decisions.context: The reference_book entity is the third of six new governance entities designed in the governance entity schema-design workstream. Its identifier prefix sets a small precedent for the cross-spec collision list. The schema-spec methodology guide's section 8 named the working assumption RB and flagged 'Reference-book identifier collision risk: RB is short and may collide visually with future prefixes' as an open methodology question for this conversation to resolve. The existing collision list at this spec's writing spans two-letter (PI, WS), three-letter (DEC, SES, RSK, TOP, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT), and four-letter (PROC, CRMC, CONV) prefixes.
decisions.decision: **Adopt `RB` as the reference book identifier prefix, format `RB-NNN` zero-padded to three digits, server-assigned on POST omission with helper at GET /reference-books/next-identifier.** The two-letter form matches the existing WS and PI short-prefix posture established by DEC-123 (which affirmed two-letter form is acceptable). The collision-risk concern from the spec guide's section 8 is resolved by checking the remaining-three governance entities' working prefixes — WT (work_ticket), COP (close_out_payload), DEP (deposit_event) — none of which begins with R or shares visual shape with RB. The existing REF prefix (references entity) is three letters and structurally distinct; the two-letter RB versus three-letter REF does not introduce visual ambiguity. The longer-form alternatives (REFB, REFBOOK, RBK) add no value beyond length. Spec guide section 6's collision list is extended with RB.
decisions.rationale: RB is mnemonic, short enough to scan rapidly in tables and references, and unambiguous against the existing prefix set. The two-letter form is consistent with the precedent set by DEC-123 (workstream chose two-letter WS) and DEC-129 (conversation chose four-letter CONV per its own judgment), confirming the spec guide's section 8 working assumption that each downstream conversation makes its own length call within the 2-to-5-letter range. The collision-risk concern named in the spec guide's section 8 turns out to be a false alarm: the remaining three governance entities have working prefixes that do not collide with RB, and the REF / RB distinction reads cleanly because of the visual-shape difference between two and three uppercase letters. The longer alternatives offered no semantic improvement and would have made every reference book identifier two or three characters longer for no payoff.
decisions.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — REFB.** Four letters. Rejected because it adds two characters with no value; the collision concern that motivated the longer form turned out not to exist. **Option (c) — REFBOOK.** Seven letters. Rejected for the same reason at twice the cost. **Option (d) — RBK.** Three letters. Rejected because the K is silent and reads as a typo rather than a meaningful character; RB is the natural abbreviation. **Option (e) — Defer the prefix choice to build-planning.** Rejected because the prefix is needed in the spec body (acceptance criteria reference RB-NNN format) and the spec's per-spec completeness gate requires the prefix locked in §3.1.
decisions.consequences: Spec guide section 6 collision list extended with RB. The kickoff's open methodology question 2 (reference-book identifier collision risk) is resolved in favour of the two-letter form. The remaining three governance entity conversations (work_ticket, close_out_payload, deposit_event) have first refusal on their own prefix choices; the working prefixes WT, COP, DEP do not collide with RB but each conversation makes its own call. The reference_book table's identifier column has CHECK constraint `^RB-\d{3}$`. The GET /reference-books/next-identifier helper returns the next available RB-NNN value.

decisions.title: reference_book versioning model: parent record plus child versions table, distinct from charter/status singleton-with-payload pattern
decisions.context: The kickoff prompt named versioning as the principal design question for the reference_book entity. Reference book files accumulate versions over their lifetimes — the methodology workstream master plan has v1.0 (05-11-26) and v1.1 (05-12-26); the methodology guide has v1.0 and v1.1; the multi-engagement architecture document has multiple versions. The schema needs to represent that version history in a way that supports the operational query 'what version was in force at the time of session NNN' (in-force-at-time-T semantics). The kickoff named four candidate models: (a) versioned-replace modeled on charter and status, where the reference_book record IS the current version and previous versions live in a parallel reference_book_version table; (b) embedded version array, where a reference_book record holds its current state plus a JSON array of version history; (c) content-addressed snapshots, where each version is a separate record with a content hash; (d) link to git commits, where no in-database versioning is done and git provides the history.
decisions.decision: **Adopt the parent record plus child versions table versioning model.** A reference_book record carries the parent metadata (identifier, title, description, kind, status, file path) plus two denormalized current-version pointer fields (reference_book_current_version_label, reference_book_current_version_date). A sibling table reference_book_versions carries one row per known version of a reference book, addressed by (reference_book_id, version_label) unique. Each version row carries version_label, version_date, optional version_summary, plus base timestamps. The parent's denormalized pointer fields stay in sync with the child rows via access-layer recompute on any child-table write — the row with the highest version_date wins as 'current'. The in-force-at-time-T query is realized by GET /reference-books/{id}/version-at?as_of=... returning the row with the highest version_date ≤ as_of. Establishes the documentary-entity versioning pattern, distinct from charter/status's singleton-with-payload pattern.
decisions.rationale: The parent-plus-child model is the lightest shape that captures version history faithfully for a documentary entity. The charter/status singleton-with-payload pattern (Decision 29) embeds the full payload in each version row, which is appropriate when the database is the source of truth for content. For reference books, the content lives in git/files — the file path string and the project's existing git history together carry the content; the database carries only version metadata (label, date, summary). The embedded JSON array alternative would require array manipulation for every version operation and would not support the version-at query efficiently. Content-addressed snapshots add complexity (content hashing, fork semantics) for benefits that real-use signal does not yet justify. Link-to-git-commits would push every query through git subprocess calls — heavier than the database, and disagrees with DEC-121's single-source-of-truth principle for governance state. The parent-plus-child model supports the version-at query directly with a single SQL clause and stays additively retrofittable (a future release can add a version_payload JSON or BLOB column on the child table if FTS or full-content queries become a real need).
decisions.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Versioned-replace modeled on charter and status.** Rejected because the singleton-with-payload pattern bundles content storage into the version row; for reference books the content lives in git/files, and bundling would create a second source of truth that could drift. **Option (c) — Embedded version array on the parent record.** Rejected because array manipulation in SQL is inefficient and the version-at query would require array scanning rather than indexed lookup. **Option (d) — Content-addressed snapshots.** Rejected as premature complexity; the project has no fork semantics need today, and the schema admits a future retrofit additively. **Option (e) — Link to git commits with no in-database versioning.** Rejected because every version query would require git subprocess calls and the V2 database would not be the source of truth for governance state, contradicting DEC-121.
decisions.consequences: Two database tables: `reference_books` for the parent records plus `reference_book_versions` for the child rows. ON DELETE CASCADE foreign key from child to parent so a hard-delete of the parent cascade-removes versions; soft-delete on the parent does not affect children (their rows persist, supporting restore-recovery). Two denormalized pointer columns on the parent — reference_book_current_version_label and reference_book_current_version_date — maintained by access-layer recompute. Three version-management sub-endpoints (GET /versions, POST /versions, GET /version-at?as_of=...) realizing the operational queries. The (reference_book_id, version_label) unique constraint admits two reference books each having their own v1.0 row. No DELETE on individual version rows in this release; versions are append-only in practice. The model establishes the documentary-entity versioning pattern for the remaining three schemas to consider; work_ticket is single-use (no versioning), close_out_payload is single-shot (no versioning expected), deposit_event is append-only (no versioning).

decisions.title: reference_book documentary-shaped lifecycle with three statuses and base timestamps only; new cross-spec precedent for documentary-vs-workflow distinction
decisions.context: The schema-spec methodology guide's DEC-126 precedent established that workflow-shaped governance lifecycles carry per-status lifecycle timestamps (e.g., workstream's workstream_started_at, workstream_completed_at; conversation's six per-status columns per DEC-131). Reference book's lifecycle is structurally different from workflow: its status states (active, archived, superseded) capture documentary outcomes rather than workflow progress. The conversation surfaced the question: does reference_book carry per-status lifecycle timestamps following the DEC-126 precedent, or does it deviate, and if so on what principle?
decisions.decision: **Adopt a three-status documentary lifecycle (active → archived / superseded) with base timestamps only (created_at, updated_at, deleted_at — no per-status lifecycle timestamps).** The lifecycle inherits two access-layer rules from workstream and conversation: (a) truly-terminal posture — both terminal statuses (archived, superseded) reject all outgoing transitions including between terminal states; (b) supersession-requires-edge rule — setting status to superseded requires an outgoing references-table edge of kind `supersedes` to another reference_book record, returning HTTP 422 {error: supersession_requires_successor_edge} on violation. Establishes new cross-spec precedent: **documentary-shaped lifecycles inherit base timestamps only**, deviating explicitly from DEC-126's per-status-timestamps-for-workflow-shapes precedent. The remaining three governance specs (work_ticket, close_out_payload, deposit_event) apply the documentary-vs-workflow distinction on their own facts: work_ticket has a workflow-shaped consumption lifecycle and inherits per-status timestamps; close_out_payload is likely workflow-shaped (drafted → applied) or append-only after apply; deposit_event is append-only with one timestamp. None of the three is documentary-shaped.
decisions.rationale: Reference book's status states do not carry per-state duration semantics worth querying. 'How long was the methodology guide in active status before being archived' is not an operationally meaningful question — what matters is when the document existed (already captured by created_at) and when its role ended (already captured by the archive/supersede transition's updated_at, with full before-and-after detail in the change_log table). Status transitions are also infrequent — a typical reference book moves from active to archived or superseded at most a few times in its lifetime, vs. a workstream or conversation which moves through multiple non-terminal states routinely. Adding six per-status timestamp columns would consume storage with no query-time benefit. The deviation from DEC-126 needs to be principled, not ad-hoc; the principle is documentary-vs-workflow, which is a clean structural distinction and a precedent the remaining three specs can apply explicitly. The truly-terminal posture and supersession-requires-edge rule are inherited because they apply regardless of workflow-vs-documentary — both express integrity constraints on status-relationship consistency.
decisions.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Follow DEC-126 verbatim with three per-status timestamp columns (archived_at, superseded_at).** Rejected because per-state duration is not an operationally meaningful query for documentary entities; the columns would consume storage with no value. **Option (c) — Use append-only posture instead of soft-delete-with-restore.** Rejected because reference book records can legitimately be created with errors (wrong title, wrong file path, wrong kind) and edit-and-correct is the appropriate flow; append-only fits decisions, sessions, and deposit_events where the rows are transactional fact, not metadata. **Option (d) — Add only one per-state timestamp (terminal_at).** Rejected as inconsistent — either follow DEC-126 fully or deviate to base-timestamps-only with a clean principle. The mixed posture would be harder to reason about.
decisions.consequences: Reference book records have eleven columns total (seven content/classification fields + two denormalized version pointers + three inherited base timestamps). Server-side enforcement of truly-terminal posture plus supersession-requires-edge rule; access-layer rules to test. New cross-spec precedent established for the remaining three governance specs — work_ticket inherits per-status timestamps (workflow-shaped consumption lifecycle), close_out_payload's choice depends on whether the conversation lands a workflow or append-only model, deposit_event almost certainly uses base timestamps plus one applied_at (append-only fact). PI-022's backfill pass does not need to reconstruct historical per-status timestamps for reference book records (none exist); the backfill records the bare metadata and the existing file's Revision Control table provides the version_date values for the child versions table.

decisions.title: reference_book_kind closed eleven-value enum covering DEC-117's seven artifact types plus three observed additions plus an other sentinel
decisions.context: DEC-117 named seven reference book artifact types as the family-1 scope: workstream master plan, methodology guide, schema specification, Product Requirements Document, implementation plan, session-startup entry document, and apply script. The conversation surfaced the question of whether reference_book_kind should be a closed enum (with future additions requiring migration) or an open string, and whether the enum should cover only DEC-117's seven or extend to additional types the project's operating history surfaces. The project's history at this conversation's writing includes documents that legitimately fit reference-book semantics but were not named in DEC-117's seven: architecture documents (multi-engagement-architecture.md), conduct framework documents (PRDs/process/conduct/*.md), and investigation reports (multi-tenancy-routing-investigation-report.md, methodology-schemas-cbm-paper-test-findings.md).
decisions.decision: **Adopt `reference_book_kind` as a closed eleven-value enum**: product_requirements_document, implementation_plan, workstream_master_plan, methodology_guide, architecture_document, schema_specification, conduct_framework, investigation_report, apply_script, session_startup_document, other. The first seven values listed above plus apply_script and session_startup_document map directly to DEC-117's seven named artifact types. The three additions beyond DEC-117 — `architecture_document`, `conduct_framework`, `investigation_report` — capture documentary types observed in the project's operating history that legitimately fit family-1 semantics. The `other` value is the sentinel for documents that do not fit the named categories; the intent is that real use will surface additional kinds, added by extending the enum in a future migration. The enum is closed because the spec wants validation to catch misclassification at insert time; the open-string alternative would admit typos and inconsistent labels.
decisions.rationale: Closing the enum lets the access layer reject typos and inconsistent labels at insert time, which protects the master-pane Kind column's scan-ability (no 'product requirements document' vs 'product-requirements-document' vs 'PRD' confusion). Eleven values is small enough to enumerate in a combo box without scrolling. The three additions beyond DEC-117 are real categories observed today; deferring them would force consultants to use the `other` sentinel for documents that have a clear category, which would degrade master-pane filtering. The `other` sentinel exists as an escape hatch for unforeseen kinds and as a signal-collection mechanism — if `other` rows accumulate, the project knows a new kind is needed. Open-string alternatives were rejected because they admit drift; the open-pattern-and-validate alternative (regex-validated kind strings) was rejected because the validation rules would be no easier to maintain than an enum migration.
decisions.alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Open-string kind field.** Rejected because it admits typos and label drift, degrading filterability and master-pane scannability. **Option (c) — Closed enum with only DEC-117's seven values.** Rejected because three legitimate kinds beyond DEC-117 (architecture_document, conduct_framework, investigation_report) are observed in the project's history; using `other` for them would degrade filtering. **Option (d) — Closed enum with twenty-plus values speculating future kinds.** Rejected as YAGNI; admit kinds as real-use signal surfaces them. **Option (e) — Per-kind tables (one table per kind, polymorphic association).** Rejected as premature schema specialization; the kinds share enough metadata (title, description, file_path, status) that one table with a discriminator is correct.
decisions.consequences: Reference_book table's kind column has a CHECK constraint enumerating the eleven values, plus a matching domain-validated set in the access layer. The master-pane Kind column renders the enum values as friendly labels (e.g., 'Product Requirements Document' for `product_requirements_document`) per the UI rendering note in spec §3.6.2. List endpoint supports `?kind=<value>` filter for at-a-glance subset queries. Adding a twelfth kind in a future release is a one-line vocab.py update plus one Alembic migration on the CHECK constraint; the `other` sentinel covers the gap until then. PI-022's backfill pass assigns one of the eleven values to each historical reference book record; ambiguous cases (a document that legitimately fits multiple kinds) pick the dominant kind and use `other` only as a last resort per the spec §3.8.2 backfill guidance.

decisions.title: reference_book field inventory, repo-relative file path semantics, no engagement-scoping flag, soft-delete posture
decisions.context: Routine items batched together at the close of the reference_book schema-design conversation: the field inventory beyond identifier, status, and timestamps; the deliberately-excluded fields; the file-path semantics; the engagement-scoping question; and soft-delete versus append-only posture. Each item follows defaults from the schema-spec methodology guide; no significant deviations were warranted, but the per-engagement-isolation question and the file-path validation rules warrant explicit decision recording because they shape access-layer behavior.
decisions.decision: **(a) Field inventory** is seven content/classification fields (reference_book_identifier, reference_book_title, reference_book_description, reference_book_notes, reference_book_kind, reference_book_status, reference_book_file_path) plus two denormalized version-pointer columns (reference_book_current_version_label, reference_book_current_version_date) plus three inherited base timestamps (created_at, updated_at, deleted_at). No per-status lifecycle timestamps per DEC-137. Notes is a consultant scratchpad — internal, not part of the user-facing summary. **(b) Deliberately excluded under minimum-viable scope**: reference_book_author, reference_book_owner (Doug is sole author/owner today; one migration away if multi-author engagements emerge), reference_book_engagement_scope (per-engagement isolation makes scoping implicit per DEC-115/DEC-116). **(c) File-path semantics**: repo-relative path string with validation rules — non-empty trimmed, no leading slash, no `..` segments, no scheme prefix (https://, file://). Path is not validated for existence at write time (file may be authored separately and the record updated to point at it); a future build prompt may add a 'check path resolves' verification. The path is resolved at use time against the consuming repository's root — typically the crmbuilder repo for dogfood records and the client repo for engagement-specific records. **(d) Title uniqueness**: case-insensitive unique within the engagement. **(e) Soft-delete posture**: default — soft-delete with restore — explicitly distinct from `archived` status (a lifecycle outcome) and `superseded` status (a successor-replaced outcome). Soft-deleting the parent does not affect child version rows; their rows persist for restore-recovery. **(f) Storage-level length caps**: none on text fields, matching workstream and conversation precedents.
decisions.rationale: Field inventory follows minimum-viable per the methodology guide; deferred fields are listed in spec §3.8.3 with growth-condition language so future signal can justify additions. Author/owner omitted because Doug is the project's sole author and adding the columns prematurely would require a backfill policy without operational value. Engagement-scoping flag omitted because V2's per-engagement isolation (DEC-115/DEC-116) makes the scoping implicit — the same logical document (e.g., the methodology guide) may exist as a reference book record in each engagement that cites it. File-path validation rules prevent common errors (absolute paths breaking on a different operator's machine, `..` segments allowing path traversal, scheme prefixes that aren't filesystem paths) without committing to filesystem-existence validation that would brittle-couple the database to the current commit. Title uniqueness scoped to the engagement matches the per-engagement isolation principle. Soft-delete fits because reference book records are organizing metadata, not transactional fact; create-with-error and correct-via-edit is the appropriate flow.
decisions.alternatives_considered: **Field inventory with reference_book_author column** — rejected for minimum-viable scope; sole-author working pattern; one migration away. **Field inventory with reference_book_engagement_scope column** — rejected because per-engagement isolation makes scoping implicit. **File-path validation including filesystem-existence check** — rejected because it would brittle-couple records to the current commit; a future build prompt may add a separate 'find broken pointers' maintenance query. **Title uniqueness globally (across engagements)** — rejected because logical documents legitimately exist in multiple engagements (the methodology guide as a reference book record in the CRMBuilder dogfood engagement and in the CBM client engagement); per-engagement uniqueness matches the isolation principle. **Append-only posture instead of soft-delete** — rejected because reference book records are organizing metadata, not transactional fact; correction via edit is appropriate.
decisions.consequences: Reference_books table has eleven columns (seven content/classification + two denormalized version pointers + three base timestamps). File-path validation runs at the access layer on POST/PUT/PATCH. Title-uniqueness check runs at the access layer with case-insensitive comparison scoped to the engagement (the per-engagement database boundary makes this trivially correct). Soft-delete on the parent leaves child version rows in place; restore reactivates the parent and the children are still there. PI-022's backfill pass populates the seven content/classification fields for historical reference book records; the backfill policy on which historical documents become reference_book records is PI-022's call per spec §3.8.2.

decisions.title: reference_book API surface plus three version-management sub-endpoints plus default UI with Kind column and inline version-history section plus sixteen acceptance criteria
decisions.context: Routine items batched together at the close of the reference_book schema-design conversation: the API surface beyond the standard endpoint set, the version-management sub-endpoints realizing the in-force-at-time-T semantics named in the kickoff, the UI layout including the master-pane Kind column and the detail-pane version-history section, and the count and shape of testable acceptance criteria. The version-management endpoints and the version-history UI section are natural additions following from DEC-136's parent-plus-child versioning model; the Kind column is a natural addition because kind is the most operationally meaningful classification for browsing the reference book catalog. None of these is architecturally novel relative to the schema-spec methodology guide's defaults but each warrants explicit decision recording because they shape API surface and UI behavior.
decisions.decision: **(a) API surface** is the standard 8-endpoint set per spec guide §3.5 (GET list with `?include_deleted=true` plus `?kind=` plus `?status=` filters; GET single; POST create; PUT replace; PATCH update; DELETE soft-delete; POST restore; GET next-identifier). **(b) Three version-management sub-endpoints**: GET /reference-books/{id}/versions (list versions in version_date descending), POST /reference-books/{id}/versions (record a new version), GET /reference-books/{id}/version-at?as_of=... (return version in force at instant). No DELETE on individual version rows in this release (versions are append-only in practice; the build-planning conversation may add a DELETE if real need surfaces). **(c) Identifier auto-assignment**: default server-side on POST omission for the parent record; version rows do not have prefixed identifiers (addressed by (reference_book_id, version_label)). **(d) Master pane** has five columns: Identifier, Title, Kind, Version (denormalized from current-version pointer), Updated. Default sort by Identifier ascending. Kind selector and status selector combo boxes in the panel toolbar drive `?kind=` and `?status=` filters. **(e) Detail pane** has nine sections in order: identifier (read-only), title, description, notes (collapsible internal section), kind combo, file_path, status combo, version-history section (subordinate panel with Add Version affordance, current version visually distinguished), ReferencesSection. **(f) CRUD dialogs** match the user-interface version 0.3 governance-entity patterns: EntityCrudDialog subclass for create/edit, EntityCrudDeleteDialog with edge-text confirmation for delete. The version-history section is hidden in create mode (one or more versions may be supplied via a `versions` array in the request body) and fully interactive in edit mode. **(g) Acceptance criteria**: sixteen testable statements captured in spec §3.7 (gate requires ten; workstream produced sixteen; this spec follows workstream's count, focused on schema migration, validation rules, lifecycle enforcement, version round-trip, in-force-at-time-T query, access-layer signatures, REST endpoint behavior, identifier auto-assignment, soft-delete and restore, sidebar position, and one end-to-end backfill exemplar against the governance-schema-workstream-plan reference book).
decisions.rationale: Standard endpoint set inherited from the methodology guide; no deviations warranted because the entity's operations follow the standard CRUD-plus-restore pattern. The three version-management sub-endpoints are the smallest set that realizes the operational queries named in the kickoff (list versions, record a new version, query in-force-at). The Kind column is a natural addition because kind is the most operationally meaningful classification for browsing the reference book catalog — consultants ask 'what's the PRD for v0.6' or 'what's the methodology guide for governance entity schema-design.' The inline version-history section is the entity's distinctive UI shape; making it a subordinate panel in the detail pane (rather than a separate dialog) keeps the version list visible alongside the rest of the record's metadata. Sixteen acceptance criteria match workstream's count and fully satisfy the spec guide's per-spec completeness gate (§7.1 requires ten); each is concrete and testable, ready for translation into test cases at build-planning time.
decisions.alternatives_considered: **API surface without version-management sub-endpoints (everything via PATCH on the parent)** — rejected because the version-management operations have distinct semantics (append a child row, recompute denormalized pointer) and forcing them through the parent's PATCH would conflate the parent's lifecycle with the child's. **Master pane without Kind column** — rejected because kind is the most operationally meaningful filter for the reference book catalog and forcing detail-pane open to see kind is high-friction. **Detail pane with version-history as a separate dialog** — rejected because the version list is the entity's distinctive shape; making it a subordinate panel keeps it visible alongside the rest of the metadata. **Acceptance criteria count of ten (gate minimum)** — would have skipped the version-management-specific criteria; rejected because the version round-trip and in-force-at-time-T query are the entity's distinctive behaviors and deserve dedicated criteria.
decisions.consequences: Eleven REST endpoints (eight parent + three child) to test; sixteen test cases per the acceptance criteria. The version-management sub-endpoints route through the access layer's recompute logic for the denormalized current-version pointer on the parent. Master-pane Kind column requires a small extension to ListDetailPanel's column-rendering (the column has a friendly-label mapping for the eleven enum values). The inline version-history section in the detail pane is a new UI pattern relative to workstream and conversation but follows naturally from this entity's versioning model — work_ticket and the remaining entities will not need it. The spec's per-spec completeness gate (spec guide §7.1) passes: all nine subsections of §3 substantively filled, sixteen acceptance criteria (gate requires ten), explicit category tags on open questions, cross-references include workstream plan, spec guide, workstream.md and conversation.md (the predecessor specifications), foundation decisions DEC-117..122, and the twelve prior-conversation decisions DEC-123..134 from SES-048 and SES-049 as inherited precedents.
